narration: Binary search requires the input list to be sorted, unlike linear search which works on unsorted lists.
Initialize the lower bound variable 'L' to 0, representing the first index of the list.
Initialize the upper bound variable 'U' to the length of the list minus 1, representing the last index.
Create a while loop that continues as long as the lower bound is less than or equal to the upper bound.
Calculate the mid index by adding the lower bound and upper bound together and performing integer division by 2.
Check if the element at the mid index matches the target value being searched for.
If the target is found, store the position and return true or print the result.
If the target value is greater than the mid value, update the lower bound to mid plus 1.
If the target value is smaller than the mid value, update the upper bound to mid minus 1.
If the loop completes without finding the target, return false to indicate the element was not found.
The algorithm works by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half to skip unnecessary values.
Ensure bounds are updated to mid plus 1 or mid minus 1 to avoid infinite loops and correctly narrow the search range.
